data.title: 5 Top Utility Stocks Powering The Global Grid
data.description:
Electric Demand Boosts Utility Stocks

Utilities have been one of the highest-performing sectors in the U.S. and across the globe in 2025 as AI-fueled energy demand lifts corporate earnings and long-term outlooks. The market has been increasingly volatile as post-government shutdown optimism fades, rate cut odds decline, and fear of an AI bubble has investors fleeing for safety. High-yield utilities are in a rare sweet spot in today’s market — offering classic defensive downside protection and exposure to soaring electricity demand.

The S&P 500 utilities sector was one of the weaker performers last quarter, with Q225 earnings growth of only +1.2% year-over-year. But the picture has completely reversed this earnings season. With more than 90% of companies now reporting, utilities are among the top three sectors in Q325, delivering an earnings rebound of +23.2%. During the quarter, top utilities unveiled new agreements to provide data centers with power, requirements that continue to burgeon. Revising its forecast upward by 10%, Goldman Sachs projects a 175% increase in global data center power demand through 2030.

Source Link

While AI data centers are a powerful demand catalyst, they will still represent only about 2–3% of global electricity consumption by the end of the decade. The broader story is the steady rise in power requirements across sectors like transportation, industrial, commercial, and residential segments. Overall global electricity consumption is forecast to reach roughly 36,602 TWh by 2030, according to the IAEA — an increase of about 20% from 2024 levels.

Not only do utility stocks stand to gain from these structural demand trends, but some may be safe havens in rough times, along with providing stable income. Utilities have outperformed during slowdowns and recessions, with demand for electricity, water, and gas remaining stable – regardless of economic conditions. I identified five utility stocks that could potentially meet these objectives for investors while helping to power the global grid.

How I Selected the 5 Top Utility Stocks Powering the Grid

I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener to identify the top quant-rated stocks in the sector and targeted securities in the electric utilities and multi-utilities industries. The utilities selected include companies that manage electricity networks and produce energy from renewable and traditional sources. The companies are headquartered in the U.S., Brazil, Spain, Italy, and Bermuda, and have operations spanning five continents and dozens of countries. My top utility picks have excellent all-around investment fundamentals, positive long-term earnings growth potential, and solid dividend yields.

1. Axia Energia (AXIA)

  • Market Capitalization: $26.32B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 1 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 1 out of 42

In a bid to establish an independent and future-oriented brand, Brazilian state power giant Eletrobras changed its name to Axia Energia and began trading under the ticker AXIA on Nov. 10. The new name, which means “value” in Greek, expresses the company's purpose: To be an “axis” that ensures predictability and sustainable performance. The company underwent a privatization process in the past three years, reducing the government ownership stake to 45%, and it has revamped its portfolio to 100% clean energy sources, including hydropower and wind, and is targeting net zero emissions by 2030.

Axia operates 82 plants, totaling 44.4 GW of installed capacity, which accounts for 17% of the national total. Axia has achieved exceptional price performance over the past year, given its strategic and financial streamlining and focus on energy storage technology, earning an A+ Momentum Grade and ranking No. 1 among quant-rated stocks in both the utilities sector and the electric utilities industry.

Axia’s Q3 net revenue fell 9.4% – largely due to the divestment of thermal and nuclear assets – offsetting higher transmission segment sales. Axia's energy trading business served 813 clients in the third quarter, up 16% YoY, with the free market segment up 18% to 723. Looking ahead, the average price of Axia’s contracted power sales is expected to rise steadily in 2026 and 2027, a trend in line with positive sentiment from Wall Street analysts. According to consensus estimates, Axia’s revenue is projected to grow by ~6.5% to $7.39B in 2025 and over 10% in each of the following two fiscal years.

Axia Consensus Revenue Estimates FY25-FY27

Axia’s A+ Growth Grade is also driven by exceptional growth in revenue and operating cash flow in the trailing twelve months. Operating cash flow is up 88% in the past year, an impressive rate compared to the sector’s 4%. Axia’s B Profitability Grade is primarily supported by a sturdy cash from operations of $3.15B, 126% above the sector median. Axia is trading at an attractive valuation with an EV/EBITDA of 9x – a discount to the sector of approximately 24%. Axia’s valuation is also supported by an 8.46% dividend yield. However, Axia’s stock has a warning indicating it might be at high risk of cutting its dividend argely due to high payout ratios and weak interest coverage. While its dividend payout timing is less consistent than the sector, the company has steadily improved over the past year.

Axia is riding a surging electricity wave and boosting clients in the free market segment while generating strong cash flow and offering a high dividend yield. The top quant-rated stock in the sector, Axia is a great option to consider for investors looking to boost the utility segment of their portfolios.

2. Iberdrola, S.A. (OTCPK:IBDRY)

  • Market Capitalization: $135.81B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 3 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 3 out of 42

Iberdrola, S.A., is a global producer and distributor of electricity, generating most of its power from renewable sources and operating regulated grids in Spain, the U.S., the UK, Mexico, and Brazil. The company has steadily repositioned its asset base toward wind, hydro, and solar, giving it long-term upside as global electrification accelerates. Over 78% of its 57.45 GW of installed capacity consists of renewable energy sources, with almost half coming from onshore and offshore wind generation.

Data centers are emerging as a major demand driver for Iberdrola. The company already supplies more than 12 TWh of renewable power annually to major tech clients through U.S. PPAs. The stock has crushed the market and sector in the past year and has continued to sustain momentum, supported by positive earnings results last month.

In the first nine months of 2025, Iberdrola adjusted net profit grew by 16.6% YoY and revenue by +2.2%, largely driven by robust operating performance in the company’s Networks business, where EBITDA rose by 26%. This was largely the result of a higher rate base driven by investment and improved regulatory frameworks, along with positive rate adjustments in the United States and Brazil.

Iberdrola’s operating profit, free cash flow, and total return on capital are key metrics driving its A+ Profitability Grade - along with impressive cash from operations of $15.02B in the past year. EPS long-term forward growth rate of 8% is underpinning a ‘B+’ Growth Grade. Wall Street analysts are expecting the company to finish the year strong, with FY25 EPS projected to rise by 22% and revenue by 17%.

Iberdrola has an average valuation grade, trading at 19x earnings, although its PEG of 2.36 and EV/EBITDA of 10.9x represent discounts to the sector of around 11% and 8%. IBDRY offers a 3.45% dividend yield, 31% five-year growth rate, and 18 consecutive years of payments. Strategically poised to ride electrification megatrends, Iberdrola’s excellent profitability and growth projections underpin a Strong Buy quant rating.

3. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. Limited Partnership Units (BIP)

  • Market Capitalization: $16.62B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Multi-Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 6 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 1 out of 20

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners owns a global portfolio of long-life utilities, transport, midstream and data assets spread across North America, South America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, and is positioning itself for rising AI infrastructure demand. The stock price has lagged the sector in the past year but has seen a surge in momentum in the last three months, boosted by strong Q3 earnings results.

In Q325, Brookfield Infrastructure recorded revenue of $5.98B (+13.38% YoY) and FFO of $654M (+10% YoY), driven by strong operational performance, organic growth, and realized gains from capital recycling activities. The company secured six new investments, including a $5B project with Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) to install up to 1 GW of power solutions for data centers and AI factories. Brookfield Infrastructure sees a huge opportunity in the AI infrastructure boom, CEO Samuel Pollock said in the earnings call:

We're also seeing a significant new growth vertical emerging from the rapid build-out of AI infrastructure, a $7 trillion opportunity set that remains in its early stages and continues to expand. We expect to deploy up to $500 million annually into AI-related infrastructure in the coming years with AI factories and behind-the-meter power solutions representing a natural compelling extension of our investment activities. These growth factors paired with a macroeconomic backdrop, that is trending very favorably, set the stage for BIP's FFO per unit growth to inflect higher.”

BIP’s A- Growth Grade is underpinned by excellent growth in operating cash flow, capex, ROE, and forward EPS growth of 13%. BIP showcases robust EBITDA and operating margins, along with capex/sales of 49%, indicating it is reinvesting at a solid rate. BIP offers a dividend yielding 4.83% with a 5Y growth rate of 5.83% and 16 years of consecutive payments. Wall Street analysts project that the dividend will continue growing steadily over the next two years.

BIP is trading at high earnings multiples, but at a significant discount relative to cash flow and revenue. Standing to benefit from soaring global AI infrastructure requirements, BIP offers profitable growth at a value, making it a top utilities stock.

4. Enel SpA (OTCPK:ENLAY)

  • Market Capitalization: $105.82B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 7 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 5 out of 42

Rome-based power giant Enel has soared in the past 12 months with momentum accelerating in the past 90 days. Enel has posted consistent earnings growth and is trading at an attractive valuation. Enel has a presence in 28 countries, a total capacity of 90.7 GW - including 66.4 GW of renewable sources - and 1.9M km of power lines in its distribution network. Enel is committed to driving the global energy transition and electrification as it targets net zero emissions by 2040.

Enel posted consistent profitable growth in the first nine months of 2025, driven by greater commodity sales and improved pricing on the wholesale market. In addition, outstanding operating performance in Latin America offset reduced margins in Italy from low hydro availability. Net income grew 5% YoY during the period, EBITDA by 1%, while total shareholder returns rose by a remarkable 76%.

The results contributed to an A+ Profitability Grade, supported by impressive cash from operations and outstanding return on equity and total capital. Negative growth in revenue and EBITDA in the past 12 months weighed on Enel’s growth scores, although EPS grew at 14% vs. the sector’s 7% – while EPS forward growth rate is at 33%. FY25 EPS is expected to rise by 4.5% in FY25 and revenue by 5.98%. ENLAY’s dividend is strong, offering a yield of 5.08%, while yield on cost is crushing the sector, for an ‘A’ Dividend Yield Grade. ENLAY’s 5Y growth rate is an impressive 7%, and it has delivered 16 straight years of dividend payments.

Enlay is trading at attractive valuation multiples, with a P/E of 12.78x representing a 34% discount to the sector, supported by solid EV/EBITDA and price/cash flow ratios, for an A- Valuation Grade. Enel is a renewable energy giant with an excellent dividend and cash flow, trading like a value stock.

5. American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP)

  • Market Capitalization: $64.88B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 8 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 6 out of 42

American Electric Power Company surged to an all-time high last month after unveiling agreements with major hyperscalers and new industrial clients to meet unprecedented power requirements. Owning the largest electric transmission system in the United States, AEP is a highly profitable utility stock with outstanding momentum and high earnings growth expectations.

AEP estimated operating EPS to rise at a CAGR of 9% through 2030 as peak system demand reaches 65 GW from 37 GW, with about 80% of growth expected to derive from new agreements with Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META), and 20% from Nucor and Cheniere LNG. AEP is well positioned to gain from the huge forecasted demand in power driven by data centers, CEO William Fehrman explained in the earnings call:

Electricity demand growth is happening, and we are seeing it play out across the country in real time. Regions with concentrated data center and industrial development, including AEP's footprint, are emerging as clear winners. Large annual capital budgets from hyperscalers totaling hundreds of billions of dollars reinforce the conviction, strength and staying power of this demand growth. At a high level, AEP's revised long-term earnings trajectory has been updated to reflect the strong load growth we are experiencing across the communities we serve.”

Although AEP’s Growth Grade is weighed down by EPS growth forward in line with the sector, the company has delivered huge growth in the past year in EPS (+37%), and EBITDA (+18%), operating profit (+27%). FY25 EPS is projected to grow by 5% and revenue by 9%. Wall Street sell-side analysts expect AEP revenue to rise steadily over the next five years, from $21.51B in 2025 to $29.07B in 2030. AEP’s A- Revisions Grade is backed by 12 upward revisions in the last three months to three down revisions. AEP outperforms the sector in nearly every key profitability quant metric, supporting an A- Quant factor grade, especially in EBITDA and net income margins, ROE, and capex/sales.

AEP’s dividend has a 3.13% yield, a five-year growth rate of 5.66%, and an amazing 36 years of consecutive payments, including 16 straight with growth. AEP’s dividend has a ‘B’ Safety Grade due to a reasonable payout ratio, good interest coverage, supported by healthy cash from operations. AEP rounds out our top five utility picks, all well positioned to ride long-term global tailwinds, powered by booming demand from data centers and AI infrastructure requirements.

Powering the Global Grid: Top 5 Utilities Stocks to Buy

Power requirements are soaring across the globe, fueled by demand from a wide range of end users and segments - including AI data centers, EVs, and national electric grids. Investors looking for exposure to these structural tailwinds should have utility stocks in their portfolios. Utilities can offer long-term upside from the electrification boom as well as a source of stable income during volatile times. I picked five utility stocks that stand to gain from these global megatrends. The companies have operations spanning five continents and dozens of countries, generating power from renewable and traditional energy sources. The stocks showcase strong collective investment fundamentals, robust profitability, earnings growth potential, and solid dividend yields.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy Investing!

data.title: Gladstone Land: Financial Review And Preferred Stock Performance
data.description:
In the following lines, we will introduce you to the company Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND), its preferred stocks, and try to assess them and give them a credit rating using Moody's approach.

Company Basic Information

  • Total assets (Q3): around $1.22 billion
  • Current market capitalization: close to $7.4 billion
  • Preferred stocks: ~$60 million
  • Debt: Close to $525 million.

Financial metrics

To assess the profitability of LAND, we examined the main profit indicators that most accurately reflect the efficiency and quality of the company's activities, noting that the results are approximate.

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): 4.02%.
  • Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt): 233%
  • Operating expenses/total revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): 27.4%.
  • Interest expenses/debt: around 4%.

We also calculate market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 0.55. We will subtract total equity from total assets and add the new one, multiplied by P/B. Based on these adjustments, we derive the market-adjusted asset yield and coverage ratio.

    • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: 5.31%.
    • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: 176.76%.
    • Historical performance:

LAND's 10-year total return on NAV is close to 5.10%.

  • Expected FFO yield: 2.6%.

The forward P/FFO is one of the highest in the sector:

Low FFO yield and high forward P/FFO are indicators of higher risk for preferred stock dividend yields. In the case of LAND, the market probably expects low FFO only for the next year, which makes the indicator inaccurate for assessing credibility.

Below are shown the basic valuation metrics:

In the case of LAND, its dividend yield is comparable to its 10-year return on NAV, and we can use it as the normal expected return from the company. This simplified approach may seem inaccurate, and it certainly is, but with common stocks, one has a lot of earnings deviation, and the confidence interval is pretty wide. This is the main reason we always prefer to look at something more predictable, such as preferred stock.

Preferred stocks

Above are shown LAND's preferred stocks - all trading below par, all with passed call dates, and all not rated by the major credit agencies.

(LANDP) has a current yield of 7.59%. It pays cumulative distributions of 6% per annum, or $1.50 annual amount, in the form of $0.125 monthly. At the IPO date (04/03/2020), the spread to the 20-year treasury yield was around 4.95%, and now the spread is close to 2.94%.

On 01/10/2018, close to 6 million shares of (LANDO) were issued. The current yield of the stock is close to 7.53%. It pays cumulative distributions of 6% per annum, or $1.50 annual amount, in the form of $0.125 monthly. The spread to the 20-year Treasury at the IPO date was close to 3.27%, and it is currently around 2.88%.

(LANDM) pays cumulative distributions of 5% per annum, or $1.25 annual amount, in the form of $0.104167 monthly. It has 0.22 years to maturity with a maturity date on 01/31/2026. It is important to note that if the company does not buy back the shares on the maturity date, there is a "penalty" that will increase the dividend from $1.25 to $2.00, or about 8% per year.

The spread to the 5-year treasury yield at the IPO date (01/12/2021) was 3.32%, and currently is 4.57%. LANDM has a yield to maturity above 8% (around 8.22%). Below is a chart showing yields to worst of the stock and potential prices. For those wondering about our yield calculations, we use the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is considered the most accurate method for ex-dividend dates.

In fact, the high yield of LANDM is pretty decent for such a short period, but we need to examine the credibility of the company.

Credibility

Since LAND does not have a credit rating, we will try to give it one using the Moody's approach.

  1. Gross assets: Ba2. The total assets of LAND are $1.22 billion (but if we adjust it with the market cap, they would be more)
  2. Unencumbered assets: Approximately 20% of its assets are unencumbered by collateral, giving it the worst rating of Cаa3.
  3. Secured debt: B2. The level of secured debt is close to 37% of the total assets.
  4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets gives us around 43% and a credit rating score equivalent to Baa3.
  5. Net debt to EBITDA: Caa3. Net debt to EBITDA. We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $60.6 million, the ratio is close to 10.
  6. Fixed charge coverage: B1 - EBITDA/(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock).
  7. Market positioning and asset quality, and operating environment: A1. We give LAND a high score here because of its historically higher valuation and low asset yield, which is reserved for high-quality, easy-to-operate assets ( from a landlord perspective)
  8. Liquidity and access to capital: B3. The operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are close to 27.4%. EBITDA covers preferred dividends and interest expenses close to 2.6 times.

The overall credit score is Ba2; if we adjust it with the market cap (common stock trading below NAV), it falls in the Ba3 range. The preferred stocks receive a 2-notch lower credit rating, or B1, which falls into the "junk" grade - high risk, which is why we avoid LAND preferred shares in general. The short term of LANDM and its failure to redeem clause may be an exception to the rule, but it is more of a personal decision.

Conclusion

Despite the high yield of the preferred stock LANDM, its high risk, supported by a B1 credit rating equivalent, makes us refrain from investing at this time.

data.title: Brilliant Photonics, Brutal Fundamentals: Why I Would Sell Out Of POET Technologies
data.description:
A New NVIDIA?

Working up and down the AI supply chains, I am coming across a wide range of new companies that can be intriguing. After dealing with Applied Materials (AMAT) and the key role it plays in atomic-precision equipment manufacturing for the semiconductor industry, I have recently learned about a company that attracts a lot of interest: POET Technologies Inc. (POET). Let me say it right away, we are in small-cap territory, since the market cap of the company is currently $588M. The company has been around for many decades (it was founded in 1972) but has changed several names and became POET in 2013. It is headquartered in Canada, but it is listed on the Nasdaq. In 2017, POET started focusing on lasers for data communications applications, and a few years later, it decided to be a "fab-light" company, as it recently explained, signing agreements with a couple of important manufacturers in Malaysia.

To understand what Poet does, let's start from where we left off and quickly recap the chip-making supply chain. We know that ASML has a unique and monopolist role: it is the only supplier of EUV lithography machines that are used by customers such as Intel, TSMC, and Samsung to manufacture highly advanced chips. Applied Materials builds the machines that are used to manufacture wafers, to deposit atomic-thick layers, and to etch. Now, Applied Materials' customers produce wafers, and here is where POET comes in because it interfaces with some of them. POET takes these wafers and builds on top of this hardware, integrating lasers and optical engines or light source modules.

Now, I am neither a physicist nor an engineer, so I had to study a bit to understand in a simple way what POET's role is.

In its 2024 Annual Report, we read that:

POET Technologies is a design and development company offering photonic integrated packaging solutions based on the POET Optical Interposer™, a novel platform that allows the seamless integration of electronic and photonic devices onto a single chip using advanced wafer-level semiconductor manufacturing techniques.

The company then highlights that since the AI revolution began, the new data centers require increasing network speeds and bandwidth. POET Technologies believes that it can play a key role as an optical module designer. This is why some have compared it to a nascent NVIDIA, which is a GPU designer. The optical engines that POET Technologies makes move an impressive amount of data at the speed of light inside an AI cluster because they make 800G and 1.6T optical links. The slide below shows the role of optical modules, which POET Technologies calls "the invisible but critical link" between "AI processors, AI software, AI service providers, and AI end-users".

Pay attention; I don't want to stretch or stress the comparison with NVIDIA. POET is not a manufacturer; it is a designer of engineering solutions based on photonics. So, the analogy holds at the level of a similar business model. Moreover, POET is in the early stages of its business and is testing early pilots without any big commercial agreements yet signed. So, when I read that POET is the next NVIDIA, I am quite cautious, and I suggest saying that, perhaps, it aims at becoming the NVIDIA for optical engines, but that there is still a lot of wood to chop to get there.

However, since the AI buildout started, the need for high-speed optical modules has seen incredibly strong demand. Here is a video that shows POET Technologies, helping us visualize a bit of the engineering behind 800G and 1.6T. POET believes that 2023 was the inflection point for 800G, which is considered to be the new speed standard for a few years (until 1.6T and 3.2T will be required).

Now, we should remember that POET designs and develops these solutions and that it has tested its first 800G and 1.6T samples. So, while the target markets are huge (data center, light source, 5G, edge computing, and sensing markets such as LIDAR are among the most popular). Now, 800G and 1.6T require extreme precision, as a company has to pack multiple lasers, modulators, photodetectors, thermal controls, and other components into a surface smaller than a fingernail. When you understand the order of magnitude, it seems that we are talking about robotic surgery, rather than industrial manufacturing. In particular, thermal management is the problem because some components heat up really fast. Right now, those who are able to produce these receivers have a head start. Probably, in the medium term, they will be the new standard, so they could be more "commoditized". But, by then, the new speed standards will have moved to 3.2T or even above this threshold. So, we have to know that this is an industry where there will always be a race to push the limits one step further.

POET Technologies' Main Financials

Let's start from the main issue that POET has. Since it is making barely any money, where does its capital come from? Mainly from investors. In October, for example, it announced the closing of a $75M investment coming from the issuance of 13.6M common shares sold to a single institutional investor. In the first half of this year, its revenues were $500k. Operating expenses over the first six months totaled $18.7M, which means that the company has a cash burn rate of around $9.35M per quarter. The balance sheet reported $73.1M in cash and cash equivalents, with no ST borrowings and no LT debt. So, right now, the capital comes from equity. This shows that the company's financial structure is backed by investors who are convinced of the business and also protects the company from the costs of debt issuance and the fluctuations of interest rates. Nonetheless, it has cash for roughly two years, unless revenues start to appear. The possible moat that POET targets doesn't therefore come from speed labels, but from the technology on how to build them. Here, POET believes that its know-how in photonics will distinguish it from the rest.

POET Technologies' Q3 Earnings Review

The company just released its Q3 earnings. Let's see if any developments are coming. After all, the stock is tanking: it topped at $9.41 in October and has now retraced back to $4.67. In the past few days, we have seen investors rotating away from highly speculative AI stocks as valuations seemed inflated and—more importantly—new rate cuts seem to be farther away than December.

Well, POET announced two initial production orders that are valued at $5.6M and believes that this is the early stage of a production ramp-up that should extend through 2026. Moreover, it introduced the 1.6T optical receiver. Non-recurring engineering revenue was $300k, while product revenue was almost none. This means that, as it stands, the company is generating some revenues from activities such as consulting. It is a good sign that it manages to get paid, but zero product revenue can sound an alarm. Now that we know that orders have been placed, we will see whether revenues will increase rapidly in the next few quarters.

In Q3, the company reported a net loss of $9.4M ($3.7M was spent on R&D), with negative FCF of $2.8M. With these numbers, it doesn't even make sense to talk about multiples. We barely have any sales to calculate a price-to-sales ratio (for those interested, it is around 410x).

The company is also seeing its SBC rapidly grow. It was $841k in Q1; it is now at $1.86M. Clearly, it has to pay its executives and talent, but this is not a trend I particularly like if revenues don't start to materialize soon. It would mean that investors are paying the executives for a project that—even though it seems to be a needed technology for the next step of the AI buildout—has found some hurdles in becoming a business.

We also heard that it closed a $250M equity financing from three institutional investors. So, we know the company will have cash at hand for quite some time, ensuring its operations for several years. This could strengthen some investors' confidence that investors believe in POET's trajectory.

As for me, I try to stay away from highly speculative stocks. I made a rare exception with SES AI (SES), because I think next year could be a breakeven point, and because its proprietary data on batteries allows it to have a very interesting platform called Molecular Universe. I also explored Electrovaya (ELVA), which I like even more because it is already profitable. But when I jump into these stocks, I keep my positions around 1% or 2% of my portfolio because it is quite common that they trade up and down double-digits in just a day or two.

When I consider POET Technologies, while I like the idea, I think the company is at a very early stage compared to the other stocks I just mentioned. Therefore, it is extremely hard to predict whether the company will be successful or not. There are risks when investing in POET, and the first and most important one is technology-related. The second one is that there is a proven commercial scale. We could call it pre-revenue risk. Cash burn is also high, and this is why we are seeing dilutions. Third, even if they manage to develop the cheaper manufacturing of optical engines, we will have to see whether they will be able to scale manufacturing together with their partners in Malaysia.

With barely any valuation metrics, the only reason one invests is whether the idea is convincing or not. For me, I have come to learn that companies in this stage can indeed deliver extremely high returns, but carry a lot of risk. I would rather stay on the sidelines and monitor the stock. If it becomes successful, there will be plenty of upside to capture. If the company doesn't reach commercial scale, investors will have preserved their capital. So, I suggest a sell rating because I think this is not the time for retail investors to take on the risk. Let's leave it to institutional investors while monitoring the company and then wait for its financials to improve so that we can run a proper valuation. Therefore, I agree with the Quant rating and believe POET is a sell.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Insight Molecular Diagnostics: GraftAssureDX FDA Submission Warrants Hold Rating
data.description:
I'm beginning coverage of Insight Molecular Diagnostics Inc. (IMDX) with a Hold rating. The reason why I believe it is important to place such a rating on this stock is because it is gearing up for two potential major catalysts this year and next year. For starters, the goal is to submit a regulatory application to the FDA for its molecular biomarker assay test known as GraftAssureDx. The goal of this testing kit is to determine relative percentage [%] and absolute [cp/mL] levels of donor-derived cell-free DNA in a person's body. In essence, the goal is to determine the probability of graft rejection after a patient has received a kidney transplant.

If everything remains on track in terms of process, then it expects to submit a regulatory application to the FDA for GraftAssureDx by the end of 2025. From there, it is not guaranteed but possible that it could commercialize this testing assay by mid-2026. The main goal for this company and inflection point would be to see whether or not it obtains FDA approval for GraftAssureDx and this remains the main inflection point for driving shareholder value. With that being said, should the FDA approve this test, then the company can streamline other assay tests immediately.

The plan is to expand the development of this assay towards the heart assay in 2026 and then afterwards the lung assay in 2027. The belief is that this company, pending regulatory approval for its GraftAssureDx, could end up targeting a $1 billion-plus total addressable market, or TAM, for kitted transplant rejection testing. With the ability to file for marketing approval of its GraftAssureDx molecular diagnostic testing kit for kidney transplant rejection by the end of 2025, plus the ability to expand in 2026 & 2027 towards assays of heart and lungs, respectively, I believe that Insight Molecular Diagnostics merits a Hold rating.

GraftAssureDx Continues To Deliver By All Accounts In Kidney Transplant Assay Testing

As I noted above, Insight Molecular Diagnostics is in the process of developing its donor-derived cell-free DNA [dd-cfDNA] testing kit GraftAssureDx. The notion of this technology is to quantify levels of a molecular biomarker to determine graft rejection after a patient has received a kidney transplant.

It is believed that molecular diagnostic testing of kitted assays could disrupt the other available molecular tests by other companies. More specifically, these other companies take a centralized laboratory approach to testing. This route is believed to be more cost-intensive and a more difficult process to navigate. On the contrary, the GraftAssureDx test assay from Insight Molecular is keen because it is taking a decentralized approach to testing. That is, to localize testing closer to the patient to not only be more cost-effective but also convenient for the patients in question. Why have I gone over this differentiation? That's because this company believes that it is going to be able to disrupt this particular market.

Consider this scenario, whereby at the present time in the United States, two companies with a centralized lab model [cost intensive and not ideal as noted above] command 90% of the market share when it comes to dd-cfDNA [Insight Molecular Diagnostics Corporate Presentation August of 2025, slide #5].

The point is that the company hopes to offer a competitive advantage for its molecular diagnostic kit assay for kidney transplant patients. That is, to offer the ability to test in-house in a far more convenient way. Plus, it's a way to save money compared to a centralized lab model.

The company's flagship technology is built on quantifying a molecular biomarker known as dd-cfDNA. This approach of targeting this biomarker for molecular diagnostic testing has been validated. However, this company is not just going to enter into it with the centralized lab testing approach that other companies have taken. Instead, it is looking at disrupting this dd-cfDNA testing assay market by taking an entirely different approach, which is decentralized testing [tests available immediately locally]. GraftAssureDx, as a decentralized monitoring diagnostic test, is considered an In vitro Diagnostic Kit [IVD] as part of the "Expand strategy" for its product pipeline [Insight Molecular Diagnostics Corporate Presentation August of 2025, slide #6]. That then leaves two other kit assays that it has as part of its product pipeline, which are as follows:

  • GraftAssureIQ - Research use only [RUO] kit assay
  • GraftAssureCore - Laboratory Developed Tests [LDTs].

GraftAssureDx, along with these other two tests, prompted a name change that the company had done back in June of 2025. Specifically, it changed its name from Oncocyte Corporation to Insight Molecular Diagnostics Incorporated to account for these strategy product name changes. In addition to its name change, it enacted one manufacturing change in play while at the same time keeping another. The change in its executive office was to move from Irvine, California, over to Nashville, Tennessee. Why on earth would it do this? This is because in Nashville it already had set up a CLIA-certified and CAP-accredited lab back in 2013. Thus, with these features already in place, it made sense to move to this particular lab to drive its mission going forward.

With respect to keeping one location the same, it would be its research and development [R&D] and lab in Göttingen, Germany. The main focus of the name change was its focus on moving towards molecular biomarker testing of transplants and the oncology sector move going forward.

The initial goal, as I described in the beginning above, is to use GraftAssureDx as localized testing for kidney transplant patients. The company believes that its disruptive nature could help it to conquer the $1 billion + addressable market for kitted transplant rejection testing. This is all encouraging, but it brings forth the possible catalysts that I was going over above. The first of which is that the company expects to submit its regulatory application to the FDA for GraftAssureDx by the end of 2025.

There was no shift in timeline because of the U.S. government shutdown, and for now this company remains focused on this particular U.S. marketing submission. Again, if the pathway of this submission process [submission and questions needing to be answered] goes well, then it should be able to commercially launch GraftAssureDx by mid-2026.

The thing is that the company still actually has some work that needs to be done to determine if the GraftAssureDx assay is going to be a better predictor of graft host rejection for kidney transplant patients. In order to accomplish this, Insight Molecular Diagnostics is in the process of running an observational validation study whereby GraftAssureDx is going to have to claim non-inferiority to standard of care [SOC] biopsies. The goal is to measure donor-derived cell free DNA [dd-cfDNA] when a person comes in for a biopsy. The trial is estimated to enroll up to a total of between 125 and 150 patients and then draw blood to be used by GraftAssureDx to detect measurable disease with a positive [+] or negative [-] call using the software algorithm in place. This data is then to be compared with SOC biopsy data and for this validation to be successful, there must be a proven non-inferiority of GraftAssureDx compared to SOC.

The primary outcome measure is going to look at two items that are considered to be cutoff points for measuring dd-cfDNA. The two necessary outcomes for the necessary assessment are:

  • Percentage of dd-cfDNA levels of 0.5%
  • Absolute values of 50cp/mL.

The point of the first assessment of dd-cfDNA of 0.5% or higher indicates donor graft injury and this number needs to remain below this threshold. The other measure incorporated into the primary outcome measure of absolute value of 50 copies per milliliter [cp/mL] has to be less than the value [<50 cp/m] like this. The reason why is because any number over this threshold might indicate acute rejection of transplanted organ.

The company has 11 trial sites at the moment, and there is definitely demand for this GraftAssureDx testing assay. The reason why I state that is because it believes that it is on track to have 20 transplant sites by the end of 2025. For now, the head-to-head graft assays seem to be comparable, but there is no guarantee that it will play out to completion.

The bottom line is that it believes its decentralized approach with its kitted assays for kidney transplant rejection will be ideal in changing the testing landscape for dd-cfDNA going forward. Everything I have gone over above doesn't even scratch the surface of expansion that might be possible in the kit assay being expanded to target validation testing of the heart and lungs. Both of which are expected to happen in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Insight Molecular Diagnostics had cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash of $20.2 million as of September 30, 2025. The thing is that this company is producing some revenues right now thanks to the sales it generates from laboratory services. That is, during this most recently reported Q3 of 2025, it noted that it generated revenues of $260,000. This was a slight increase from the comparable year-ago period, whereby it generated revenues of $115,000. The truth is that this number should not be monitored for two reasons. The first reason is that the goal of the company is to have its lead flagship product, GraftAssureDx, tracked for sales.

Thus, it has stated in its 10-Q SEC filing that it is still a pre-revenue company because of this. The second reason is because of the strategic shift from moving towards laboratory revenues over to the dd-cfDNA biomarker molecular diagnostic testing of GraftAssureDx; this is going to be the driving force for revenues to be generated going forward. The reason for the cash on hand is because it was able to enact an offering back in February of 2025. After expenses, it was able to generate approximately $28.7 million.

The truth is that this company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand. What makes me say that? Well, it is because of what it notes specifically in its 10-Q SEC filing, which is that it believes it only has enough cash on hand for a cash runway to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. The company burns roughly $11.048 million per quarter.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Insight Molecular Diagnostics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of its lead flagship dd-cfDNA assay, GraftAssureDx. The risk here is that there are still plenty of steps that need to be done before it is able to receive U.S. marketing approval of this diagnostic testing assay. For instance, the goal is to submit a regulatory application to the FDA by the end of 2025 so that it could receive U.S. marketing approval of GraftAssureDx by at least mid-2026. Even if a submission is achieved for this testing assay, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to approve this application in its present form.

The second risk to consider would be before a regulatory application can be submitted for GraftAssureDx. As I noted directly above, the company is in the process of running an observational validation study, whereby this specific assay test must achieve non-inferiority of SOC biopsy testing. The risk with this is that there is no assurance that GraftAssureDx will be able to meet this primary efficacy outcome measure for starters. If this validation assay test fails to achieve non-inferiority over SOC biopsy testing, then this could put an end to the development of GraftAssureDx going forward.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of software validation testing of its GraftAssureDx in vitro diagnostic [IVD] device. The thing is that this company is relying on its vendor for the device to be considered "software ready," and thus without this being obtained, Insight Molecular Diagnostics might not be able to submit its regulatory application for GraftAssureDx by the expected timeline of the end of 2025. In essence, the projected filing of the regulatory application could be delayed because of a vendor partner not delivering on their promise to achieve their end of the bargain.

The fourth risk to consider would be trial questions from the FDA. What this company has to do is continue to collect samples for the ongoing 11 sites it has in play at the moment. Plus, the expectation is that it is going to have 20 trial sites by the end of 2025. The risk and point I'm getting at here is that despite the company doing everything perfectly, the FDA is going to potentially have questions that need to be answered. Thus, there is no assurance that the company will be able to provide accurate answers to the FDA. In addition, even if the questions are answered, it is quite possible that the timeline towards FDA approval in mid-2026 could be pushed further up to another time period.

The fifth and final risk to consider would be in terms of Insight Molecular's financial position. The reason why I state this is because even though it raised approximately $28.7 million in February of 2025, its expected cash runway is not that sufficient. Consider that it believes its cash on hand is only going to be enough to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. What this means is that it will likely need to raise cash in the next several months, and this could put investors at risk of dilution. There is no assurance that the company will be able to raise a significant amount of cash, nor that the funds it does obtain from a transaction will be done so on acceptable terms.

Conclusion

Insight Molecular Diagnostics has taken a drastically different approach, and it shifted its focus on its lead flagship product of dd-cfDNA biomarker molecular testing with its assay GraftAssureDx. As I noted above, its revenues for its laboratory services are going to tick down because of this shift in strategy. Whether or not its name change and/or shift in strategy towards this particular assay test pays off remains to be seen. However, I believe it is important to give this stock a Hold rating for now based on the progress it has been able to make to date.

In essence, the inflection point that will determine this company's future is going to be whether or not it has the validation testing assay necessary to move towards an FDA filing. Again, should everything go as planned in terms of its expected timeline, then it believes it will be able to file a regulatory application of GraftAssureDX for transplant rejection testing of the kidneys by the end of 2025. The beauty of this company is that it is already in the process of thinking about what other assay tests it could expand to. With that being said, it is looking to expand the testing of dd-cfDNA of the heart in 2026 and then lungs in 2027.

data.title: S&P 500: A Chart Bears Love To Misread
data.description:
S&P 500: Chart of the day

The chart the title refers to is the one provided below. If you have been following analyses on major market indices (including the Dow Jones (DJI), S&P 500 (SP500), and NASDAQ (COMP:IND)), chances are that you have seen this chart (or some variation of it already). It is a very neat chart, and consequently a chart that bears love to use these days as all equity indices hovering around at-time-high prices and valuation multiples. In case you are seeing it for the first time, this chart illustrates the 10-year rolling return of the S&P 500 for the next 10 years (the vertical axes) with respect to its return in the past 10 years (the horizontal axis). The data used in this chart span from 1995 to the present. The plot is generated for the SP500. But if you reproduce the plot for other indices such as the Dow or the Nasdaq, you will more or less get the same picture.

The messages from the chart, in most investors’ interpretation (especially the bears’ these days), are so clear that they are almost irrefutable. The most common interpretation is that the chart demonstrates the powerful ideal of reversion to the mean, as reflected by the strong negative correlation via the descending red trendline. When prior returns are low, such as near -5% on the X-axis, future returns have been significantly higher historically (in the 15%+ range). Conversely, following extremely high returns, such as those in the 15% to 20% range, the future returns have historically compressed toward the 5% to -5% range.

This reminds of the old adage from Mark Twain: it ain’t what you don’t know that gets you into trouble. It’s what you know for sure that just ain’t so. Next, I will explain why the above interpretation is misleading in several ways. As such, I don’t recommend you base your asset allocation decisions on the “correlation” shown in this chart or other similar charts.

Bears ignored the basic concept of sample size

To start, the above chart gives the misleading impression that there are many distinct data points, which is the statistical foundation for any correlation. The time period used in the chart spans 30 years, thus resulting in a total of 360 data points in the chart. This seems like a sufficient number of data points. But the caveat is that they need to be unique data points to be statistically significant.

But they are not. Rolling 10-year returns rely on overlapping data—heavily overlapping data, in my view. Nine out of ten years of rolling returns in a given period overlap with the next period on an annual basis. On a monthly basis (which is how the chart above is generated), the overlapping issue is even more severe—119 out of 120 months of rolling returns in a given 10-year period overlap with the next period. As a result, many—if not most—of the data points shown in the chart above are heavily correlated among themselves and really. The fitting of the red trend line treated them as unique and uncorrelated observations, thus producing very misleading results.

Here is the revised chart, with the above overlapping issue removed. In this chart, data points were only computed for non-overlapping periods, as I was taught in statistics 101. We ended up with far fewer data points, and correlation disappeared in tandem.

Bears also ignored the basic concept of outliers

Another basic statistical concept the chart missed is the role of outliers. 101. Another lesson I remembered from statistics 101 is that before any data fitting, the outlier data points should be removed. But unfortunately (or fortunately—depending on your perspective), in investing, the returns are disproportionately impacted by outlier data points and events.

At a very macroscopic level, a few extreme events defined the equity market return disproportionally in the past 3 decades, a period which the above is based on. The top 3 on my list are the formation and burst of the dot-com bubble (roughly between 1997 and 2002), the Global Financial Crisis (roughly between 2007–2009), and the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent stimulus response (started in 2020 and is still ongoing in my view).

These three events disproportionately dominated many 10-year rolling return windows in the past 30 years. A rapid V-shaped recovery (like the one after the COVID-19 pandemic) or an extreme bubble (like the dot-com bubble) can impact many adjacent 10-year rolling return windows, creating the misconception of a trend. But in fact, it is just one outlier event being plotted many times on the same chart. As a specific example, the green oval in the chart shown below highlights a group of data points. If you examine these data points closely, you will see that they are almost all due to the formation of the dot-com bubble (which is why they feature some of the best past 10-year returns). As such, they should be considered as one data point only in my view, and the data point should be marked as an outlier. When you repeat this exercise for the other 2 events, you will end up with a lot fewer data points (more or less like the chart in the previous section), and several of them should be considered outliers.

Other risks and final thoughts

Finally, application of the reversion to the mean in the stock market is fundamentally flawed for several reasons. The concept is a more advanced concept (beyond statistics 101 anyway), and I will refrain from detailed elaboration. The bottom line is that the concept ONLY applies for statistical variables stratifying certain requirements; whether the stock returns satisfy these requirements is debatable. And my view is that they don’t for many reasons. The dominant role of extreme events mentioned above is a good reason.

Further, the mean itself is not static in the stock market in my model. Reversion implies a return to a fixed, long-term average, but the "mean" is constantly evolving. The underlying driver of stock returns is corporate earnings growth, which is influenced by technological advancements, productivity gains, global trade, etc. None of these factors remain constant or fluctuate randomly (see the example of the role of global trade in the next chart below). In other words, these fundamental driving forces for stock returns have no mean to revert to in my mind. If you agree with this notion, you can easily see why I don’t believe there is a means for the stock market to return to either.

Let me conclude by clarifying what my thesis is as well as what it is not. My thesis is to debunk the predictive power of past rolling returns for future rolling returns. The popular charts used to support such predictive power (like the one that opened this article) are flawed in fundamental ways and ignore basic statistical concepts. However, my thesis is NOT to argue that the current equity market is attractively valued. The lack of predictive power from these charts does NOT automatically mean the equity market will have attractive future returns.

In my model, the future returns of the stock market rely on a combination of its future valuation (which is uncertain) and also the fundamental driving forces of corporate profit growth (such as technological advancements, policies, and global trade). I believe technological advance will always move forward, but policies and global trade could suffer near-term setbacks. This article is already getting long-winded; I should stop here and leave further elaborations for another day. Let me just end by reiterating my caution—don't make your investment allocation decision based on the seemingly elegant correlations shown at the beginning of this article.

data.title: Sigma Lithium: The Winter Is Almost Over
data.description:
Introduction

Sigma Lithium Corporation (SGML, SGML:CA) continues to move in the right direction, with a focus on operating costs and cash flow management as it executes capacity expansion that, under current lithium concentrate prices, should result in over 45% EBITDA margins in 2026 that can greatly de-risk the company and propel the share price. I updated the YE26 price target to US$11 from the previous target at US$9.

Lithium Prices

It's been a rough two years with the collapse of lithium prices that not only gave back the pandemic spike but have dropped to below cost for many of the Chinese and Australian hard rock miners. The August capacity shutdowns in China warned the market (battery/automakers) that depressed price levels were not sustainable, and if OEMs wanted to avoid shortages and price volatility, prices needed to rise. The most recent price in the Shanghai metal exchange is at US$1,000 a ton for concentrate, which covers cash cost for most of the mines and should be the base for the market going forward.

3Q25 Results Green Shoots

While results were below expectations, on lower volumes and realized prices than estimated, but a significant improvement vs 3Q24 and 2Q25, the main positive was the cash flow generation on reduced working capital (supplier terms) that reduced net debt and helped de-risk the financial picture. The primary positives given by the CEO were the improved commercial outlook, with most of the production contracted and an improved cost base on mining efficiencies (new machinery and elimination of outsourcing), which should set the company up for positive results in 4Q25 forward.

Operating Model

I revised the operating model to factor in the 3Q25 results and, more importantly, higher realized price for 2026 to US$1,000 a ton vs US$900 and also a lower unit cost in 2026 vs the US$600/ton. The YE27 estimates have 50k tons of higher volume sales. These changes result in a 48% EBITDA margin in 2026 vs 33%.

Financial Estimates

Given the modestly higher lithium prices and lower cash costs, the financial results should benefit, and I estimate a jump to positive EBITDA and cash earnings (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) in 2026. Sigma is highly leveraged to lithium prices, and if they stay at the US$1k/ton level, the company can fund its capex and, in 2027 forward, generate free cash flow of over US$90m, and if they meet production ramp-up, FCF can be more than double in 2028.

Valuation

My price target of YE26 is upgraded to US$11 vs US$9 on the improved lithium prices and margins that drive cash earnings estimates to US$118m in 2026. Valuation multiple is unchanged at 10x P/CE (cash earnings), which is at a discount to much of the sector, albeit the peer comps may not have the same cost structure and need even higher lithium prices to attain similar margins as can be observed in the peer comps table.

Risk

As with most commodities, the main risk is lithium prices; if they drop below US$700/ton, then Sigma will burn cash, may not be able to fund capex, and could even be unviable. The demand/supply dynamics of lithium are highly dependent on China, its battery and EV makers.

Conclusion

I rate Sigma a buy. The company has navigated the lithium winter well, with a focus on operating efficiency and cash flow optimization while expanding capacity. This is set to pay off if the lithium price stays at the current US$1k/ton level, which can drive the share by multiples long-term.

data.title: Adobe: Fighting Generative AI With Share Repurchases
data.description:
Generative AI has created its share of winners and losers. But what about the stocks in between? Adobe Inc. (ADBE) continues to churn out respectable top-line growth backed by best-in-class profit margins. But investors appear worried that headwinds from generative AI are around the corner and hard to shake off. The company has been aggressively repurchasing stock, even allowing its net cash position to dissipate. The stock valuation looks attractive, allowing for solid returns even if top-line growth decelerates meaningfully. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

ADBE Stock Price

I last covered ADBE in August, where I rated the stock a Strong Buy as one of the “last few buys in software.” The stock has declined by around 6% since then.

There still is no visible catalyst to overhang the generative AI overhang, but I see more upside ahead.

ADBE Stock Key Metrics

ADBE is an enterprise software juggernaut known for products like Photoshop and Illustrator. Generative AI and its image-generation capabilities have cast doubt on the future of ADBE’s relevance, leading to a dramatic re-rating downwards. That, however, has not stopped the company from posting strong results. In the most recent quarter, the company delivered 11% YoY revenue growth to $5.99 billion, exceeding guidance of between $5.875 billion and $5.925 billion. Non-GAAP EPS of $5.31 exceeded guidance of between $5.15 and $5.20.

Current remaining performance obligations ("cRPOs") grew 13% YoY, possibly suggesting that there is no sudden drop-off in revenues expected in the near term.

The company ended the quarter with $5.94 billion in cash versus $6.2 billion in debt. The company has reduced its net cash position due to aggressive share repurchases.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see 9.2% YoY growth in revenue to $6.125 billion. As usual, I expect guidance to prove at least slightly conservative.

On the conference call, management highlighted that their “AI-influenced ARR has now surpassed $5 billion, up from over $3.5 billion exiting fiscal year 2024” and that this has already surpassed their prior full-year target. I suspect that this disclosure of AI revenues has not quelled investor concerns about potential disruption from generative AI, as investors may have been hoping for strong AI revenue growth to lead to an acceleration in overall revenue growth (which has not come to fruition). I note that management was asked regarding their confidence in being able to sustain double-digit growth, to which they answered that they are “not providing targets for next year.” I interpreted their response to indicate that investors may need to prepare for the likelihood that revenue growth ends up going lower and not higher.

Is ADBE Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, ADBE traded hands at just around 14x forward earnings. That is a steep discount to the 5-year average valuation of around 32x earnings.

Shares were selling for around 5.4x forward sales, also representing a large discount to the 5-year average valuation of around 12x sales. Consensus estimates are already factoring in a dip below a double-digit top-line growth rate.

The re-rating lower makes sense given both the deceleration in growth rates as well as the deterioration in the growth story. Whereas ADBE was once viewed as a rare tech stock that could balance both aggressive top-line growth and robust profit margins, it is now facing threats from generative AI and competitors. That said, I am of the view that the re-rating is overdone. The company still maintains a fairly conservative balance sheet, and all cash flow is going towards share repurchases, taking advantage of the low stock valuation.

While I expect some disruption to the smaller customer base, I expect the vast majority of ADBE’s business to remain resilient as customers are likely to continue wanting best-in-class software tools. I note that ADBE may be able to offset some of the headwinds through continued uptake of its AI and agentic tools. Given the high profit margins, the stock looks attractive to me even if top-line growth decelerates to the 4% to 6% range. I can see the stock re-rating to around 20x earnings over time, at the very least. Between the 7% earnings yield and top line, I can see this stock delivering 10% to 13% annual returns based on these conservative assumptions. The key here is to keep expectations moderate (something that I may not have done a good enough job of in the past), as shaking the generative AI risk overhang may not be so easy here.

ADBE Stock Risks

ADBE is facing potentially disruptive threats from many sources. Figma (FIG) continues to post strong growth and, from the outside looking in, appears to be at the very least highly competitive in their direct competition. Generative AI might enable customers to reduce their use of design-editing tools. Generative AI might help competitive products narrow the competitive gap. Generative AI might lead to mass layoffs, which would hurt seat-based vendors like ADBE.

ADBE Stock Conclusion

ADBE is not as exciting as fast-growing AI stocks, but the cash cow is happily consuming the deeply discounted shares. I expect the impact from generative AI and competition to prove more moderate than feared. The stock is already pricing in pessimistic scenarios, and I expect the stock to benefit from some multiple expansion. ADBE is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Docebo Continues Downward Revenue Growth Trend (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Docebo Inc. (DCBO) recently reported its Q3 2025 financial results, beating consensus estimates for both top line revenue and earnings.

I previously analyzed DCBO in March 2025, downgrading the stock to a Hold outlook due to a dimming revenue growth outlook.

I’m further downgrading DCBO to a Sell on a continued lower revenue growth rate, no meaningful upside catalysts in the near term and unpredictable results from its U.S. federal market efforts.

Docebo’s Market And Approach

Docebo is a pure play provider of corporate learning management systems.

According to a 2025 Grand View Research market research report by Grand View Research, the global market for e-learning was approximately $300 billion in 2024 and is expected to reach $842 billion by 2030.

If accomplished, this growth rate would be a CAGR of around 19% from 2025 to 2030, which is a very strong expected growth rate.

The primary drivers for this high forecasted growth rate are an increasing need for employee and student upskilling, hybrid work arrangements at a meaningful percentage of organizations and the ongoing requirement to improve cost efficiencies.

The chart below shows the expected growth path of the worldwide E-learning market through 2030 and by region:

A number of competitive e-learning industry players are listed here:

  • Instructure

  • McGraw Hill (MH)

  • Absorb LMS

  • Udemy (UDMY)

  • Coursera (COUR)

  • Saba Cloud

  • SAP SuccessFactors Learning (SAP)

  • Udacity

  • Tovuti LMS

  • 360Learning

  • Cornerstone OnDemand

  • SumTotal Learning

  • Captivate Prime.

Docebo derives 71% of its total revenue from the United States and almost all of its revenue by type from subscriptions, as the pie charts below indicate:

Docebo’s go-to-market efforts are primarily led by its direct sales approach, but it also has partnerships with integration partners such as Workday (WDAY) and via channel partners like Vartopia and Bluewater.

Also, it targets users directly via its online platform with scalable pricing tiers to meet customer demand, depending on size and usage.

DCBO’s nearest competitor is likely Instructure, which was acquired by KKR (KKR) in November 2024 for an EV of $4.8 billion, a 16% premium to its pre-announcement enterprise value.

Industry trends in the corporate LMS space include integrating AI and automation-enhanced features to help companies improve their employee skills, improved analytics for business impact measurement and mobile-first/remote worker learning environments that can be accessed anywhere and anytime.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary market characteristics:

Strengths

  • Strong position in the corporate LMS market, revenue stability from subscription model, worldwide footprint, emphasis on AI features and modern learning modes.

Weaknesses

  • Sliding growth rate, smaller scale than other players, dependence on retention and expansion of existing customers.

Opportunities

  • Expansion into new verticals (government), geographic expansion, increasing demand for external training.

Threats

  • Intense competition from many LMS providers, need for continued R&D enhancements, slower enterprise sales cycles on larger prospects.

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen due to mid-market outperformance and EMEA region strength. Operating income by quarter (red line) has improved because of greater SG&A leverage.

The degree of operating leverage has turned down from my previous Q4 2024 report, primarily from a Q1 2025 negative result; otherwise, it has improved in the most recent quarter:

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has moved lower as a result of a mix shift toward professional services and onboarding costs. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have dropped and management is expecting a further decline on greater efficiencies. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have reflected continued investment in its generative AI integration efforts.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have bounced back from an early 2025 plunge.

(All data in the above charts is IFRS.)

For balance sheet results, DCBO ended the quarter with $66.1 million in cash and equivalents and no debt.

The company's Rule of 40 performance has been trailing unevenly downward in recent quarters, as the chart shows here:

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was a reasonably robust $30.3 million, and capital expenditures were a very low $1.1 million.

The company paid $6.1 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $24.2 million.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at a hefty $44.4 million, indicating the company is retiring stock at a good rate.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 2.6%, a fairly low figure for a company like Docebo.

The company continues to invest in its government efforts and platform enhancements.

Overall, Docebo’s capital allocation is shareholder-friendly on disciplined SBC, minimal CapEx and strong stock buybacks.

Why I’m Bearish On Docebo

In the lengthening aftermath of the pandemic period stock price runup due to that period’s sharp growth, Docebo’s stock has performed disappointingly, as the firm’s growth rates have trended lower over time.

While the industry has seen recent developments with AI-enhanced learning feature growth, the result has been a need for dedicating R&D spend on those enhancements.

In the end, it’s difficult to see major differentiation from those changes, at least among the more forward-looking vendors in the LMS space.

Management’s current growth strategy involves continued focus on middle-market opportunities, growth in U.S. federal and SLED as a result of its FedRAMP certification, and AI-enhanced product features that improve product differentiation and upsell impetus.

However, U.S. federal contract progress may be slowed by volatility in administration priorities, although the company says that the recent shutdown hasn’t materially affected its pipeline progress.

The potential benefit of its U.S. federal efforts is that once it gains acceptance and use in a particular department, it is more difficult to dislodge, although revenue wins may be lumpy from contract wins or losses.

I’ve prepared a chart showing the frequency of negative-oriented keyword mentions in the most recent conference call of management with analysts:

The graphic illustrates that the company is continuing to see "deal elongation" in the commercial market, as companies exert deal scrutiny in an uncertain macroeconomic environment.

Docebo also saw a greater Dayforce white label/OEM relationship wind down effect on revenue, which should continue through 2026 but become immaterial in 2027.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing DCBO at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 2.5x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 12.9% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. DCBO not providing this important figure.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 4.9x on October 31, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, DCBO is currently being valued by public investors at a significant discount to the broader index as of October 31, 2025.

Looking ahead, management is guiding to a topline revenue growth rate of 11.4% in 2025, significantly lower than in past years.

It is difficult to see a meaningful catalyst for the stock in the near term, since topline revenue growth is slowing from its Dayforce wind down and other market factors.

While the corporate e-learning market is expected to see robust growth in the coming years, DCBO appears to be losing market share due to its lower comparative growth rate.

It may gain some U.S. federal business, but even that is lumpy and difficult to predict.

As such, my estimation is that investors may be able to earn better rates of return on their capital elsewhere, so I’m downgrading DCBO further to a Sell.

Market Review

The global economic landscape remained broadly stable through the third quarter, though underlying fragilities persisted. Most major central banks held interest rates steady, with the exception of the Federal Reserve, which delivered a widely anticipated 25bp cut and the Bank of England, which also cut rates by 25bp. Inflation trends varied: modest increases were seen in the US, UK, and China, while Japan experienced a gradual easing. Growth data across developed markets showed modest expansion, though generally below long-term trends, and business confidence indicators remained mixed. Labor markets, particularly in the US, showed signs of softening, while fiscal pressures intensified in Europe, culminating in the collapse of the French government over budget disagreements.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the Fund’s Class I shares (SDGIX) returned 1.24%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate USD Hedged Index, returned 1.21% for the same period.
