Market Review (continued)

Trade tensions escalated as the US resumed tariff hikes following the expiry of a 90-day pause, imposing steep duties on goods from China, Canada, Mexico, Switzerland, and threatening India over Russian oil imports. These measures injected uncertainty into global trade flows. Geopolitical tensions remained elevated but less dominant, with a brief and inconclusive meeting between Presidents Trump and Putin in Alaska regarding the Ukraine conflict. Overall, while global growth held steady, inflation, trade policy unpredictability, and political instability continued to weigh on the outlook heading into the final quarter of the year.

There was some divergence across government bond markets in the third quarter. Despite concerns about fiscal expansion, the Federal Reserve eventually cut interest rates amid signs of a softening economy and weaker labor market conditions. US yields were lower with the 10-year Treasury yields declining 8 basis points (bp) to 4.15%. Elsewhere, government bond markets were generally weaker as yields rose. In Germany yields trended upwards, although not uniformly, with 10-year yields ending 11bp higher at 2.71%. Meanwhile, 10-year UK gilt yields rose more substantially as concerns about debt levels and the sustainability of the deficit increased, reaching 4.70%, an increase of 22bp. In Japan, government bond yields also increased. The 10-year yield exceeded 1.6% for the first time since the global financial crisis, rising 22bp to 1.65%. With US Treasury yields having fallen, yield levels in the emerging markets also declined, with the JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index ending 8bp lower at 5.93%.

Credit markets continued to make gains relative to governments as spreads tightened towards new multi-year lows through the quarter, despite an apparent lack of much positive global economic momentum. Both investment grade and high yield markets outperformed governments. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) over governments for the Bloomberg US Corporate Index ended the quarter 9bp tighter at 74bp, with the related Bloomberg US Credit Index also 9bp tighter at 70bp. The spread on the Bloomberg Euro Corporate Index also tightened by 13bp at 79bp. The Bloomberg Sterling Agg Corporate Index was 15bp tighter at 86bp. In high yield markets, the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index spread was 23bp tighter at 267bp, while the Bloomberg Pan-European High Yield Index spread tightened by 38bp to 265bp.

Performance Review

Duration was a tailwind on the quarter, with an overweight to the US being a driver of the positive return. Curve positioning yielded additional positive alpha with strong performance from a Japanese flattener and UK flatteners more than compensating the negative performance of a US steepener. Cross-market trades also outperformed, spurred by overweights in New Zealand versus Australia and Canada versus the US, but an overweight in the UK versus the US detracted. Active positioning in US Agency MBS outperformed. On the quarter, the primary headwind to performance was our underweight in credit beta, while our preference for euro denominated investment grade credit versus its US dollar counterpart contributed. Active foreign currency positioning yielded a positive contribution to performance, with an underweight in New Zealand dollar and Swiss franc being its primary catalysts. Active inflation positioning yielded additional if modest alpha.

Market Outlook

The global growth outlook remains underwhelming, with expansion rates generally in the 1% to 2% range in the major developed market economies, which is almost universally a sub-par rate. Elsewhere, China is struggling to achieve the target growth rate it sets for itself. Inflation is expected to remain sticky and above central bank target levels in some places. Further rate cuts are expected to be dependent on inflation expectations and whether labour markets slacken or remain relatively robust. The primary wildcard is the effect that US trade tariffs will have on growth and inflation. We believe the US Federal Reserve will ease policy further before reaching a neutral rate, with 2026 facing the uncertainty of who is appointed the new Fed Chair. The Bank of England is likely to ease rates once more, while the ECB may have completed its policy easing for this cycle. The Bank of Japan faces entrenched inflationary pressure and is expected to tighten policy further, albeit gradually. Political uncertainty remains elevated with leadership elections in Japan, ineffectiveness in France and the shutdown in the US. In the US, some macro indicators suggest there is resilience within the economy, but other factors such as the softening labour market align more with further slowdown. While we continue to believe that a recession is unlikely, it does remain a clear risk if the effects of tariffs and immigration crackdowns squeeze demand further. We anticipate GDP growth of 1.6% for 2025 as a whole, with similar sub-trend activity again in 2026. Inflation is still above the Fed’s target and the effects of tariffs are likely to maintain upward pressure on prices if businesses do not or cannot absorb them. However, the weaker labour market may help prevent an unruly escalation in inflation levels. Having eventually resumed its easing cycle after nine months, the Fed appears to be turning its attention as much to the labour market as to tariffs, and we see further cuts to come that may take the Fed funds rate to the 3.25% to 3.5% range. Treasury yields are expected to ease, with the 10-year yield declining below 4%, towards 3.85% in 12-months’ time. The US government shutdown will potentially lead to weaker sentiment and, until resolved, reduce the availability of data produced at a federal level.

In the eurozone, the manufacturing sector, which has been under pressure for some time, remains weak. Positive real wage growth in 2025 could support consumption, but subdued consumer confidence offers little cause for optimism the consumption will accelerate. On the positive side, the prospect of a large German fiscal package adds some upside risks to our growth forecast, while the US tariffs add significant downside risk via both the trade and uncertainty channels. Eurozone inflation has been around 2% for some time but we forecast it will modestly fall next year owing to base effects. The effect of tariffs will be nuanced; any retaliatory tariff price hikes may be tempered by softer demand and China could look to Europe as an alternative market. We do not expect further rate cuts in 2025, though there may be one more cut in 2026. Bond yields are not expected to diverge greatly from current levels for the time being. Politically, in France, difficulties in forming a government continue. As we expect little progress in the near term given the polarised nature of the French parliament, the situation leaves political risk elevated.

In the emerging markets, China’s economy appears to be stabilising but ongoing domestic issues, notably in the property sector, remain a hurdle to achieving the growth trajectory the authorities would like. Tariffs and global trade tensions are combining with the underlying demographic factors, such that we believe the 5% target for GDP growth will be missed both this year and in 2026. Given the subdued growth outlook, we expect inflation to remain low for some time. The Chinese authorities are expected to intensify fiscal and monetary support, aiming to stimulate domestic demand and the property sector. Any interest rate cuts that the People’s Bank of China implements will likely be only modest. GDP growth rates in other emerging market countries are relatively robust, but many still face the effects of US tariffs with little prospect of them being lifted soon. As with most developed market economies, inflation is expected to continue moderating slowly over time, though there may be volatility in that process. Interest rate cuts may also be forthcoming, particularly if the US continues to give ground and the Fed cuts rates further.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer's ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio's other investments.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Unhedged) is a flagship measure of global investment grade debt from twenty- four local currency markets. This multi-currency benchmark includes treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed-rate bonds from both developed and emerging markets issuers. J.P. Morgan Market Bond indices are a family of benchmarks designed to track the performance of various bond markets, including those in the US, emerging markets, and global markets. These indices cover a range of asset classes and currencies, providing investors with a way to track and compare the performance of different bond sectors. The Bloomberg US Corporate Index is a benchmark for the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable US corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg Euro Corporate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that tracks the performance of the investment-grade, euro-denominated, fixed-rate corporate bond market. It includes securities from industrial, utility, and financial issuers publicly issued in Eurobond and eurozone domestic markets. The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. The Bloomberg Sterling Aggregate Corporate Bond Index is a fixed-rate, investment-grade Sterling-denominated corporate bond index that tracks the performance of the fixed-rate, investment-grade Sterling-denominated corporate bond market. It's part of a broader family of fixed income indices offered by Bloomberg. The index includes bonds from industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg Pan-European High Yield Index tracks the performance of fixed-rate, high- yield corporate bonds issued in Europe, denominated in EUR, GBP, CHF, DKK, NOK, and SEK. It specifically focuses on non-investment grade debt, excluding emerging market debt and certain other debt types. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS) is an investment similar to a bond that is made up of a bundle of home loans bought from the banks that issued them. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments similar to bond coupon payments. A yield curve is a line that plots yields, or interest rates, of bonds that have equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country’s borders in a specific time period. Inflation is the rate of increase in the cost of living. Inflation is usually quoted as an annual percentage, comparing the average price this month with the same month a year earlier. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the measurement of the spread of a fixed-income security rate and the risk-free rate of return, which is then adjusted to take into account an embedded option. A basis point (BP) is a unit of measure used to indicate percentage changes in financial instruments. Basis points are typically expressed with the abbreviations "bp," "bps," or "bips." One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%. In decimal form, one basis point appears as 0.0001 (0.01/100).

data.title: CoreWeave: This Isn't The Bubble Pop, Yet
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) stock has cratered post Q3 earnings, down 24% over the past five days, adding to last week’s bloodbath and bringing the stock down 42% over the past month. Needless to say, markets have been feeling the heat, and CoreWeave hasn't been an easy name to own.

This comes after CoreWeave had already underperformed the market during 3QCY25, declining 16% versus the S&P 500, up 8%. Now, CoreWeave is down 43% since our last article in early October titled “Something is Very Wrong.” The stock was priced for perfection, and any slip-up would’ve brought the market down crashing, and there was no shortage of triggers, including Michael Burry’s bearish take on Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir, the government shutdown, and angsty reaction to tier 1 raised CapEx and OpenAI (OPENAI) committing $1.4T in AI infrastructure deals.

This quarter, CoreWeave bit the bullet: management trimmed FY25 outlook to $5.05B-$5.15B from $5.15B-$5.35B, trailing estimates for $5.29B, and cut CapEx to $12B-$14B, substantially lower than $20B-$23B previously expected. CoreWeave said the reason for the downward revisions is:

temporary delays related to a third-party data center developer behind schedule.

The customer affected by the delays is willing to wait it out, so there is no hit to the total value of the original contract, but everything is pushed out into FY26. Q4 CapEx is now expected to fall into Q1, with management eyeing higher CapEx, at least double, in 2026, stating that

“given the significant growth in our backlog and continued insatiable demand for our cloud services, we expect CapEx in 2026 to be well in excess of double that of 2025.”

What does Q3 tell us?

This isn’t CoreWeave's first post-earnings crash; last quarter, the stock also took a nosedive after the print due to the higher-than-expected quarterly loss and operating expenses jumping 275% from a year ago quarter.

For Q3, operating expenses were $1,312,826, higher than last quarter’s $1,193,579 by 10%. Revenue, similarly to Q2, grew triple-digits Y/Y to revenue of $1.36B, ahead of the expected $1.29B and loss of $0.22 per share. The company ended the quarter with a backlog of $55.6B, adding about $25B during the quarter largely due to the deal with Meta Platforms (META) and OpenAI. The problem for investors, aside from the stock being priced for perfection, is the fact that in spite of such a hefty backlog, management is cutting FY25 guidance, and keep in mind we still have little visibility on the 2026 growth outlook.

To us, the Q4 outlook and the lowered guide for FY25 are less reflective of a bubble pop moment and more reflective of a new product ramp that comes with a headache.

Investors should remember that a big part of why CoreWeave became Wall Street’s darling so quickly is its cozy relationship with Nvidia and the latter’s 7% stake, and that gets CoreWeave to be first in line for Nvidia’s next B300/GB300 product line, shown below. The ramp of B300/GB300 is trickier than markets are expecting because it is all new, and the industry is still figuring out how to make the tech itself work without overheating or liquid-cooling leaks. So, we think this is why CoreWeave took such a financial hit this quarter; the products aren’t ready due to delays in the supply chain, and so the financials are lagging behind.

Our longer-term concern for CoreWeave is that its business model is inherently flawed, because it'll require continued high upfront CapEx to offset the risk of tech obsolescence. We get into this in more depth here on our post-IPO thoughts on CoreWeave's business. This is not the reason we believe the stock is crashing now; instead, we view this sell-off as market panic over the impact of delays amid heightened AI bubble concerns. There will be a time to run from CoreWeave, but we don’t think this is it yet.

The company still has an active power of 590 MW, with the goal of 850 MW of active power by year’s end. The total power with contracted capacity is over 2.9 GW, which makes CoreWeave an industry pick for capacity lease agreements, particularly from enterprise and hyperscalers. The company has +1 GW of contracted capacity available to come online within the upcoming 12-24 months. Remember that big picture, CoreWeave is still the only neocloud with a Platinum ranking from SemiAnalysis. The fact that we’re also seeing CoreWeave’s customer concentration issue ease is a positive signal for us, given that, as of Q3, no single data center provider accounted for more than 20% of contracted power.

Bottom Line:

CoreWeave stock has shed over 40% of its value over the past month. RSI is deepening into oversold territory currently at 23, as shown below. Valuation has come down slightly, although the stock continues to trade at an AI premium. Forward EV/Sales is down from 16.25x in early October to 11.18x. We believe the stock has priced in the short-term negatives and see outperformance in 1H26 as AI-driven anxiety eases and the B300/GB300 ramp turns more successful.

The stock is trading at $77 per share at current levels, down from its high of $187 per share and CoreWeave is the worst performing neocloud on the YTD, 6M, 3M and 1M chart. The chart below is CoreWeave's six-month chart.

We think the reality is that CoreWeave is a high-risk, high-reward pick. Investors are circulating more money into newer bitcoin-mining turned AI infrastructure plays, and confidence in CoreWeave is wanning.

At current values, we think the bulk of the negatives have been priced into CoreWeave's stock price and outlook for FY25. A lot weighs on the broader AI narrative and continued concern over overspend without guaranteed return on investment. We say the bulk of the negatives, because we expect Nvidia's Q3 earnings report next week (November 19) to potentially weigh on expectations due to potential delay issues with the B300/GB300 ramp. We believe what markets are now digesting is that CoreWeave's positioning is a double-edged sword, and we see room for outperformance as expectations reset.
